translation: By the fig and the olive
commentary:
وَٱلتِّينِ وَٱلزَّيْتُونِ

I swear by the fig and I swear by the olive.

وَطُورِ سِينِينَ

And I swear by Mount Sinai.

وَهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ ٱلْأَمِينِ

And I swear by this peaceful land.

These opening ayahs present four oaths that serve a profound purpose. Before exploring their meaning, we must understand that when Allah ﷻ takes an oath in the Quran, He is not simply highlighting blessed objects. Rather, He is directing our attention toward a critical message that follows. The oath is preparation for the subject.

This peaceful land clearly refers to Makkah, as we've seen in earlier surahs with the phrase هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ ٱلْأَمِينِ. Mount Sinai is unmistakably the mountain where Musa (AS) spoke with Allah ﷻ. But what about the fig and the olive?

These are not merely references to blessed foods. In Arab tradition, regions were often identified by their most prominent produce. When we consider that two of these oaths are clearly locations—Mount Sinai and this sacred land—it becomes apparent that the fig and olive also represent places. The most compelling interpretation is that At-Tin and Az-Zaytun refer to Jerusalem and its vicinity, the land associated with Isa (AS) and the prophets of Bani Israel. The Bible itself mentions that Isa (AS) spoke on the Mount of Olives.

Looking at the structure, these oaths trace a remarkable prophetic geography. They begin where Ibrahim (AS) left his children—in the land of the fig and olive—then reference Mount Sinai in the middle, representing Musa (AS) and the entire history of the Israelites with their many prophets, and conclude where Ibrahim (AS) settled his other family, in Makkah, which would eventually witness the coming of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

These are the lands of revelation. The Torah, the Injil, the scriptures given to Ibrahim (AS), and the Quran—all descended in these regions. But more than that, these locations carry the entire story of revelation: how it was given, how people responded to it, what happened to those who believed and those who rejected. Each place represents not just a prophet, but a complete narrative of divine guidance and human choice.

When someone visits Makkah today, they're not just seeing a city—they're standing where Ibrahim (AS) once stood, placing stones together, making dua for this place to be a land of peace. Similarly, Jerusalem and Mount Sinai carry profound historical weight. These locations make us conscious of Allah's ﷻ extended relationship with humanity, His continuous effort to guide us through His messengers.

translation: We have certainly created man in the best of stature
commentary:
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ

We have certainly created the human being in the most upright fashion.

This ayah presents a statement that might seem to contradict our daily experience. If you ask the average person whether humans are fundamentally good or evil, many would lean toward the negative. Modern psychology, influenced heavily by figures like Sigmund Freud, often assumes we are deeply flawed from childhood, constantly trying to "untwist" our disorders. When you watch the news, read history, or even reflect on your own experiences with people, it's easy to conclude that humanity is fundamentally broken.

Even the angels questioned this reality. When Allah ﷻ announced His intention to place a khalifah on earth, they asked: "Will You place therein one who causes corruption and sheds blood?" Their observation wasn't wrong—look at what humans have done throughout history, and their comment seems remarkably accurate.

Yet Allah ﷻ makes a counter-statement here that is both profound and hopeful: We created the human being فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ—in the most beautiful, most upright fashion. The word تَقْوِيمٍ comes from قَامَ, meaning to stand upright and firm, balanced and not leaning to either side.

This continues the theme we saw in earlier surahs about the nafs being perfectly balanced—وَنَفْسٍ وَمَا سَوَّىٰهَا—the soul and how it was proportioned. Allah ﷻ placed competing forces within us: emotions and intellect, personal needs and family obligations, individual rights and social responsibilities. We have tendencies that pull in opposite directions, yet we're given the capacity to balance between all of them. This is the remarkable nature of Allah's ﷻ creation.

The word أَحْسَنِ emphasizes this isn't just balance—it's the most beautiful balance. And notice that تَقْوِيمٍ is indefinite, suggesting this balance manifests at every stage of our lives. As we age, Allah ﷻ grants us the balance appropriate to that age.

Some scholars even noted the physical dimension of this uprightness. Humans stand upright naturally, unlike most creatures. The Quran itself asks: "Is one who walks fallen on his face better guided than one who walks upright on a straight path?" Our physical posture reflects our spiritual potential.

Consider also what Allah ﷻ protected most carefully in our bodies. Our skull, made of the thickest bone, protects the brain. Our ribcage, equally strong, protects the heart. These two organs—the mind and the heart—are the sources of our uprightness, and they receive the greatest physical protection. Even this is part of أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ.

The connection to those opening oaths becomes clear now. Those remarkable messengers—Musa (AS), Ibrahim (AS), Isa (AS), Muhammad ﷺ—they are forever recorded in history as proof that humans can reach extraordinary heights. They were not supernatural beings. إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ—"I am only a human like yourselves," they said. Their examples demonstrate what humanity is truly capable of when living up to the potential Allah ﷻ built into us.

Yes, humanity is often disappointing. But that doesn't mean we're not capable of greatness. The story of those lands proves what amazing taqwim humanity possesses, especially as modeled in those messengers.

translation: Then We return him to the lowest of the low
commentary:
ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَـٰهُ أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ

Then We reduced him to the lowest of the low.

Here comes the tragedy. The word رَدَدْنَـٰهُ means to cause something to change from its original state to something lesser, to deteriorate. The Quran uses this root elsewhere: "And among you is he who is returned to the most decrepit old age." It describes capabilities starting to fall apart, strength that was once there beginning to deteriorate.

But wait—if Allah ﷻ says "We reduced him," doesn't that make it sound like it's Allah's ﷻ doing and not our fault? This is where the grammar becomes fascinating and reveals deeper meaning.

There's an apparent grammatical issue here. In Arabic, when you have a superlative like أَسْفَلَ (lowest), it should be followed by a singular word, not a plural. It should be أَسْفَلَ سَافِلٍ. But the ayah says أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ—lowest of the low ones (plural). This creates a deliberate tension that offers two profound meanings.

First interpretation: Of all creatures that could be considered low, human beings descend even lower than them. We don't just become low—we become lower than animals. Think about it: a crocodile kills when it's hungry or threatened, then stops when its stomach is full. But humans? We kill even when we don't need to. We demonstrate greed that no animal shows. We're capable of evil that transcends even animal instinct. People walk into schools or theaters and kill indiscriminately, and we can't even explain why. This is أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ—lower than the low.

Second interpretation: The word إِنسَـٰنَ can be singular or plural in Arabic. Reading it as plural, the meaning shifts: "We allowed them to deteriorate to a state as low as they themselves were acting." In other words, their own state was already low, and Allah ﷻ let them continue. You want to be lowly? Fine. You've got it. The doors are open—go ahead and descend further.

This is similar to the Quranic expression يَمُدُّهُمْ فِى طُغْيَـٰنِهِمْ—"He extends them in their transgression." You want to go further into error? Go ahead. Do more wrong. Descend even lower. Humiliate yourself even more.

When philosophers wander from one philosophy to another or people jump from one form of empty entertainment to the next, the Quran describes them as descending into valleys: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ—"Do they not see that they wander in every valley?" When you descend into a valley, gravity helps you down, but there may not be a way to climb back up.

Some scholars interpreted أَسْفَلَ here as a reference to Hellfire, since the Quran uses similar language: إِنَّ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ فِى ٱلدَّرْكِ ٱلْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ—"Indeed, the hypocrites will be in the lowest depths of the Fire." However, this doesn't fit the context. The more coherent reading is that Allah ﷻ made human beings with amazing uprightness, and they allowed themselves to become low. Allah ﷻ simply permitted them to go as far down as they wanted.

translation: Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds, for they will have a reward uninterrupted
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ

Except those who believed and did righteous deeds; for them is a reward uninterrupted.

After describing the tragedy, Allah ﷻ offers hope. This exception can be understood in two complementary ways.

First: There are human beings who became very low, but there's a separate group who didn't descend—those who believed and did good deeds. They remained high. They lived up to the potential Allah ﷻ described in أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ. How do you fulfill that potential? When you come to iman and accept revelation, when you do righteous deeds—that's when your humanity is completed and fulfilled.

Second: Even among those who became very low, there are some who decided to believe and rose again. They became the exception among the people who had fallen. They were in depths of darkness, then they took shahadah and became like the companions—people who lived evil lives before Islam, then Allah ﷻ guided them. Think of Umar (RA) or Khalid (RA)—they would say of themselves that they were among أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ, but Allah ﷻ gave them a chance to rise again through the gift of iman.

Now let's examine this common Quranic phrase more carefully: ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ—"those who believed and did righteous deeds."

The word آمَنُوا۟ comes from أَمْن, which originally means safety, security, and peace. To believe, to have iman, actually means to be at peace—no longer at war with Allah ﷻ and what He wants from you, no longer at war with your own nature. You've accepted peacefully what Allah ﷻ asks of you, and you're at peace with yourself. This is the essence of believing.

When you were previously at war with truth, clearly damage was done. Corruption happened. The opposite of فَسَاد (corruption) is صَلَاح—to fix, to correct, to rectify. So when Allah ﷻ says وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ, He's not just saying "do good deeds." The word الصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ means actions that correct wrongs that were done in the past, or a life in which you're constantly examining: What did I do that wasn't right? How can I fix it? What needs to be made better? This is the act of صَلَاح.

The word عَمِلُوا۟ is significant too. عَمَل means to do something consciously, deliberately—as opposed to something that happens automatically or subconsciously. Right now you're breathing without telling yourself to inhale and exhale. Your eyes see without being commanded. But speech, hand motions, chosen actions—these are conscious acts.

What Allah ﷻ is saying: Once you become at peace with yourself and with Him, you become conscious about what needs to change in your life and what needs fixing.

And beautifully, it's not an endless list. Notice that الصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ is grammatically a plural adjective, but it's the form used for a limited number—just a few good things. Look out for injustice. Don't consume haram. Fulfill your duties to Allah ﷻ. Just a handful of critical matters—look out for those, and you fulfill the conditions of your iman.

The word وَ (and) could even be understood as "namely"—they believe, namely they act in ways that correct wrongs. Their actions are proof of their belief.

These are people meeting the potential Allah ﷻ gave them. They have that nafs inside that is balanced. Every time they see something out of balance, they put it back in balance. That's proof they are people of أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ. They're living up to that great gift Allah ﷻ bestowed upon them.

About them, Allah ﷻ says: فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ—they will have a compensation that is غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ.

The word مَمْنُونٍ has two meanings. One meaning: something imposed on someone, reminded of, made to feel obligated about. Like when someone gives you something then constantly reminds you: "Remember that favor I did for you?" Allah ﷻ is saying their reward in Jannah will never make them feel like they're receiving a handout or that favors are being held over them. They will be honored and dignified. Even though we say مَنَّ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْنَا—Allah has favored us—He doesn't impose that favor in Jannah.

But I prefer the second meaning. The word مَنّ in Arabic was used for the rope tied to a bucket lowered into a well. This rope needs to be strong and reliable, because you don't want to lose the bucket down there. A rope that's unreliable, coming apart at the seams, ready to snap—that weak rope is called مَنّ. Something مَمْنُون has the tendency to fall apart, to not last, to be cut off.

Allah ﷻ is saying: their reward will never be cut off, never discontinued, never flake on them.

Why is this significant here? Throughout history, people visit monuments, castles, towers, ancient palaces. The reward of those places—the legacy of those who built them—is مَمْنُون. Eventually it's cut off, forgotten, turned to dust.

But you go to Mount Sinai, or Jerusalem, or Makkah—there are no grand castles there marking those prophets. No huge armies. Nothing material. Yet the reward of those people is غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ. It continues. There's no cutting it off.

Think about what lasts as a human contribution. People live their lives wondering: What will be my lasting impact? What will I leave behind? The king leaves palaces. The wealthy leave companies. And when they have nothing else, they build giant monuments to their own graves, or donate wealth right before death with the condition: "Put my statue at the entrance."

Allah ﷻ is saying all of that is مَمْنُون—it will be cut off. The only legacy that lives on is your good deeds.

These locations Allah ﷻ referred to in the opening—the good that those prophets did—the fact that you and I are believers today is a continuation of the good they did. Their reward lives on through us. And if we become part of that chain, our reward will live on through the generations after us. This is what's worth living for. This is the evidence of history. This is how you avoid the tragedy so many human beings have suffered.

translation: So what yet causes you to deny the Recompense
commentary:
فَمَا يُكَذِّبُكَ بَعْدُ بِٱلدِّينِ

So what could make you deny the judgment after this?

This ayah poses a hard question, and it can be understood in three ways.

First interpretation: Allah ﷻ is addressing the Prophet ﷺ. يُكَذِّبُكَ refers to others denying him. The meaning: "Who still calls you a liar about the judgment—about the religion—after all of this has been said, after contemplating all of this evidence?" It's a rhetorical question expressing amazement that anyone would still reject after such overwhelming proof.

Second interpretation: Allah ﷻ is addressing the human being directly, challenging him: "What makes you lie against the judgment now? What compels you to still lie to yourself about the religion that's coming, the judgment that's been passed, that was given to so many prophets before you whose history you know, whose story you've been informed about? How can you still be this way after being created in أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ?"

Third interpretation: The question isn't about lying, but about what empowers someone to argue. "What argument do you have left now against this judgment, against this verdict of Allah ﷻ?" Do you really have a counter-argument after hearing all of this? After understanding the purpose of your creation—to rise to your humanity? Don't you see yourself having become so low, so immoral, so materialistic, so wretched? يُكَذِّبُكَ—is there nothing in your conscience saying, "I have no evidence against this. I have no argument left"?

translation: Is not Allah the most just of judges
commentary:
أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَحْكَمِ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ

Is not Allah the most just of judges?

Before we look at the meaning, understand the word حَكَم. Its root refers to something tied together, woven tightly. When someone is skilled at weaving, all the threads are perfectly balanced, colors aligned, lines straight, stitching precise—that's حُكْم.

From this root comes حِكْمَة (wisdom)—arguments well-stitched together, thoughts and ideas organized and coherent, not scattered. Wisdom isn't just information; it's beneficial knowledge that's structured and useful.

From the same root comes حُكْم (judgment, verdict)—and حَاكِم (judge). Why is a judge called حَاكِم? Because a true judge makes judgments based on coherent arguments and wisdom. When he passes a verdict, it's founded on حِكْمَة. So we have: حَاكِم (judge/ruler), حَكِيم (wise), and حُكْم (judgment)—all three meanings are stitched together from one root.

This is also a word used to describe the Quran itself: كِتَـٰبٌ أُحْكِمَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ—a Book whose verses are perfectly composed. It means the Quran is full of wisdom, full of wise verdicts, and perfectly stitched together—all three meanings simultaneously.

Now to the ayah: أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِأَحْكَمِ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ—"Is not Allah the wisest of all rulers? Is He not the most suitable and wise of all judges?"

The بِ here adds emphasis, an exclamation. You could say أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ أَحْكَمَ without it, but the بِ intensifies the question.

What's the connection between this question and the previous one? The first question was: "What argument do you have left to deny?" And this follows with: "Isn't Allah the best judge?"

What Allah ﷻ is saying: Are you seriously questioning your messenger? Are you questioning the religion? Do you realize you're not just questioning the din—you're questioning Allah ﷻ Himself? You're not just opposing a human being standing before you. Those people in those regions thought they were merely opposing a messenger. They weren't. They were opposing أَحْكَمَ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ—the wisest and most suitable of all judges and rulers. That's who they were up against.

The Prophet ﷺ used to respond to this ayah, even in prayer, saying: "Of course You are—You are the wisest and most suitable of all rulers and judges."

## Structure and Cohesion

Let's appreciate how everything in this surah is stitched together:

**Part 1 (Ayahs 1-3):** The lands where revelation descended—where Allah's ﷻ wisdom and judgments came down. The land of the fig and olive, Mount Sinai, this sacred land. These are the places where دِين and حِكْمَة descended.

**Part 5 (Ayahs 7-8):** Who denied the din before? The people in those very lands where it descended. This is where Allah's ﷻ verdicts came. So the surah calls upon that historical evidence: "Are you still going to argue against this overwhelming proof from history?"

**Part 2 (Ayah 4):** Made for greatness—لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِىٓ أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ.

**Part 4 (Ayah 6):** When is that greatness truly achieved? When we rise into it—إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ. When faith leads us to deeds that rectify matters, we receive compensation that's never discontinued. This is when we live up to أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ.

**Part 3 (Ayah 5):** The tragedy in the center—ثُمَّ رَدَدْنَـٰهُ أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ. People who reduced themselves to the very lowest.

Look at the structure: At the top and bottom is the legacy of revelation. Second and second-last is the legacy of believers achieving their purpose as human beings. And in the absolute center is the failure of humanity—أَسْفَلَ سَـٰفِلِينَ.

The surah converges toward the middle, surrounding human tragedy with divine hope and historical proof. This is how Allah ﷻ, أَحْكَمُ ٱلْحَـٰكِمِينَ, weaves together His message—each thread perfectly placed, each meaning supporting the next, all of it leading us back to the question: After all of this, what argument could you possibly have left?
